Okay, we're going to take a side trip. True story. I used to sell in type of videos door to door. And we used a number of models. Sometimes we would just knock on the door, fold. Sometimes we would have leads that were supplied by the company. Sometimes we would call and make telephone appointments and so forth. I want to tell you how it worked with telephone. Because there was a formula that if you kept at this word, that formula would prove out to be true. And I may not remember exactly, but I remember it pretty closely. As a general rule, it took three presentations inside the home to sell one set of in type of videos. The goal, our main goal of salesman back then was to make three presentations every night that we worked because we'd sell one set of in type of videos. And we'd make approximately $100, which is probably the equivalent of $600 for today's market. We'd make those three presentations. Generally, we would have to have eight appointments. We would make eight appointments. We'd go out and knock on those eight appointments. And the way it would work is that four of us, four of those people, would tell us to each shit and die. One of them, the other four would let you in and go to the front off. And three out of the eight would let you make your presentation. And of those three, we would make one sale. Now, obviously that is not absolutely precise, but in general that's the front sale. To get those eight appointments, we had to dial about 70-some odd telephone. Because we found that we dialed 72 numbers. We'd get about half 70-some numbers. We'd get about half of them to answer. We'd make the calls during the day. And we would get half of them to answer and a certain percentage of those would blow us off. And another percentage would allow us to make the appointment, etc. And usually it was the wife that said, yeah, you could come out. And then about 50% of the time when the husband comes home, said, what are you doing letting the encyclopedia salesman come out? That's the half that would burn you off and you'd get in to the other half. Now, there was this guy named Eimakstromite. I'll never forget. He was up in Michigan. He was a sales manager. And here's what he used to do to train these people. First of all, there is a very carefully orchestrated sale that you use in your selling and collecting. It is written, and I think a better word is choreographed, by some of the most savvy psychologists in the world. And it's a show and tell kind of thing where you have broad sheets that you lay on the floor and you have dummy books that you set up and it's just formula. And they used to teach, you know, they said, stay with the formula. Don't add anything. Don't delete anything. And now, these 30 years later, I really understand the wisdom of that. Before I tell you about Eimakstromite, I'll tell you about another guy who was my salesman. His name was Duane Harnes. And Duane was a photographer's assistant who got fired one new year's day for some Mickey Mouse thing, and he got this job with Americana selling in psychopheles. And when he would go out to sell in psychopheles, he could memorize the pitch at first. And so he carried it with him, printed out, and he would say, Mr. and Mrs. Jones, I just want to make sure that I don't forget to tell you about any other thing you're entitled to. So I've got it all written down and he would read it to me. Seriously, he would read it to me. That guy was making 700 bucks a week in 1960. Now, getting people to sell in psychopheles is a tough thing. It's hard to recruit them. I don't even know if they do. And they do. People still sell them during the week. And to keep them, it's pretty tough too. So here's how Eimakstromite is handled back. He would have his, I guess, I don't know if you'd call him students or whatever, the people who was training, his trainees come in and make the telephone appointments for him. They would do, make those 70-some dials until they got the 30-some answers, until they got the 16 conversations that led to the 8 appointments or whatever. And then that person would accompany Eimakst out in the evening to knock on the doors. And there in front of his training, he would make hundreds of dollars a week, never allowing the trainees to talk or get any part of the info. And you know what happened? The way it worked in trainees' minds? This guy is making money from my lead, my appointments, right? And by the time he let these people loose, it would, you know, they would wham, wham, wham, they'd just go out and sell, sell, sell, just like that. And it was that buildup that let it go. So that's why I'm telling you, don't, when you take a break, you want to take a break at the right time. You want to take a break after you've done all this prep work. Now, there will be a time that will come to you when you just, you'll know when it's right for you to sit down and start right. Okay? And I just promised you, you will know. Now, what I'm about to tell you is crucial. What you do is you get your legal pad, if that's the way you write, that's the way I write, and you get your typewriter, if that's the way you write. And you start writing, and you write it all in one session, as fast as your little arthritic hand will race across that page. You start writing as fast as you can write. You don't worry about spelling, you don't worry about grammar, you don't worry about any Gary Halberts rules of punctuation, indentation, spaces between paragraphs, nothing. You just race it right through, race it through. You're blinding, burning, you know, just smoldering piece of raw coffee down in that page. And don't hold back anything. No sensors at all. Say anything that you want to say. Be as lurid and as graphic. Don't deliberately try and go overboard with spilt every piece of enthusiasm you have onto that page. Just, and do it fast. Fast is a key. A lot of people think that really good writers that, you know, they're sitting there and they type and then they go over every word and they come back and they smooth it out, polish it and shine it up, etc. Well, there's some of that involved with the stuff that's interesting to me reading is written in a white, hot key. You've all read some of my newsletters. They're not all equal in entertainment or information value, nor should they be, because if they were all tense, I would never push the envelope. But you've all read some of them where you just can't put it down, right? You just start going, zoop, zoop, zoop, you know, it's a wham, wham, wham, wham. Those are the ones I write just like that. I write them just like that. Because it's been on my mind and it's been building up. Someone said, how long does it take you to write your newsletters? I said, it takes 57 years. That's right, it takes me generally 57 years and an hour and a half. So what you do is you write that really, really fast. And then, what I said to you, you mean how long will it be before you start to write? Okay, what I said to you is you'll know when that is. That's a very good question. Let me tell you when it isn't. It isn't 15 minutes later. It should take you, it should be about three days, gentlemen. Let this digest for about three days. Now you've done an awful lot of stuff now and you've done a lot of it right to get your story on paper, right? You've done a lot of it right. Now, three days, you've let the three days go by and spilled it out on paper, okay? And it is now time for you to chill again for a little while. Okay, now, then you take of all the letters that you picked out that make the best template letter. And you sit down with the one that you wrote when you were on fire. And you have all your other material in your index cards and your transcripts and everything in front of you. And more importantly, you have it all ingrained in your mind. Now we are going to sit down and we are going to impose some sort of order into this. What we have here, okay? So the first thing we're going to do is we're going to write a headline. And you will have a collection of 60-some headlines or whatever it is in this collection. And I have another product I published some time ago that has 349 headlines in it. Now what you want to do is you want to read the letter that you wrote while you were on fire. Reread all of your index cards. Reread the transcripts of your tape conversation. And among all everything else, there will be something that will leak out at you, which is if you only have 20 seconds, you could only say something for 20 seconds. You could only say one thing, that's the thing you will say. Probably in this one it will be something like this, take this simple test and in 72 hours you'll never be tired again. I don't know that that would be it, but there's something there that's going, something about what you're selling is going to stand out among, from everything else. It's going to be hotter than anything else. And that is what we will fashion into our headlines. Now the way, once we have that written down and firmly in mind, whatever it is, they'll start reading all the headlines that you copied in your own handwriting or you typed out. And it's like I have literally done this myself, okay? I have these headlines typed out. I've written all of these. I mean I didn't originally write all of this. I originally wrote a bunch of them, but I did hand write all of these before they were typed. Now I'm going to read some of these headlines to you and I want you to listen. What do you think is the main benefit of taking the test that you have and finding out what your through-dialogue is on? Relief. Relief from pain? Relief from something that bothers someone. That's crazy. Relief from pain. Relief from understanding and knowing pain that goes away or reduces. Okay, now I'm going to do something and I want you to watch what happens to you and the room as I do this. We're going to, whether it's accurate or not, we are going to accept for the fact for the moment that that is the number one thing, okay? And I'm going to start reading headlines to you. And you don't necessarily have to verbalize it or write it out, but every time I read a headline, I'm going to pause. And when I pause, if you can then think of a way that that could be twisted to say something on belief in pain, I just want you to raise your hand, okay? It will. It will. It just will. That's how you get your headlines. Now, having done what we have just done is we have already written the most important thing that we're going to write in this letter. We have come up with a, we have identified and zeroed in on our target's most vulnerable part. And we have fired a laser missile with a nuclear warhead right into that part of his psyche, right? So now, unless we mess everything up, we're going to pull this off. Carlton writes the best bullet. There's one headline that I love that I think was, well, it proved itself to be so irresistible. And some of you may know about it, but it's worth a little, it's worth a little bit of, it's worth, I think, telling the story of how it came about. I worked for quite a long time with Ernest and Tova Board Nine. And Tova Board Nine had a cosmetic company. They had a cosmetic company called Tova Nine. And their main product was this cactus mask that you put it on and it tightened up your face and softened it and made you look younger. And that's the one that became the amazing facelift in the job used by Hollywood stars who don't want plastic surgery. A big part of that success of that ad was the fact that we could credential it with Ernest Board Nine's celebrities. I can assure you none of the women bought the product because they wanted to look like Ernest Board Nine. In fact, we put his picture in one of the ads and it diminished the full of it. But anyway, one of the things that Tova wanted to do is come out with a perfume. And she traveled all over the world trying to get a perfume developed to her satisfaction. And went to all these little guys included. She was dancing around often, not gaffes, does violence, and never got anything the way she wanted. So one day I was reading to the National Enquirer and there was an article explaining that how all perfumes are made from essential oil, water, and sometimes a little alcohol. And if you know what essential oil is used in your favorite perfume, instead of paying $150 an ounce, you can make a pint of it, you know, truthfully. A couple of bucks. And the popular perfumes back then probably still popular ones, you know, were Chanel number five and both of them and stuff like that. And it told you what the essential oil was to make them. That's interesting. So I was in Westwood, California and there was a little kiosk of an outdoor vendors place. They're around now, by the way, and this woman sold like little tests to vials of essential oil. And what would happen is that the women would do exactly what the National Enquirer was suggesting. You know, they would go get an essential oil, mix it up with a little, that would become their perfume. So I went to, I asked the girl there, I said, is there any of these essential oils? There's a couple, two, three hundred of them, that women like above all else. And she says, well, when anybody really tried them all, the five far, the hands down favor is something called China Musk. I said, really? I said, why doesn't someone take China Musk and put it in the bottle and call it ABC perfume? She said, gee, that's a good idea. I'm going to drop it out. So I bought some China Musk and I went to a jewelry and I got a special little bottle made and had the name Tova etched on it. And I poured the China Musk into there, stopped the bottle back up and made a really neat package out of it. And there was a photo shoot where Tova was being photographed and Ernie was being photographed too. And I happened down there and that's where the picture of Ernie and I got together. When I went in there, Tova was there and I said, look at this. She said, what's that? I said that she was in the fragrance. And she was tense right then because now she was going to have to disappoint me. She was going to take the grip of this and say, well, it's interesting. It's something like women say when they really mean, you know, get the hell out of my life. You know. I like this. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. I want you to know that I really respect the ability to fly. Thanks. But anyway, so she takes a grip of this and goes, whoa, she almost screwed this. And she says that's perfect. That's exactly what I'm looking for. What's the formula? What's this made of? I have to keep that a secret, you know, because it involves a lot of research and stuff. So we decided that we're going to put this on the market. And she wanted to hold the perfume launch. And there was a woman named Candy Stone who at that time was married to Erin Stone, the producer of all the television shows. And she had this boutique on Beverly Hills and she was going to hold it for theme launch. And I said, Tova, if you let me do this the way I want to do it, let me do the advertising the way I want to do it. We'll never get the people in there. She said, well, what kind of place do I need? I said, you know, the central closet of the dollar. She said, you kid, I said, no, you've got to have the central closet of the dollar. By that time, I had made them so many millions of dollars that she just bit the bullet and rented the entire bottom of the central closet of the dollar. And some of you know this, but some of the stories you don't know. I then wrote a headline that was so powerful, it got 7,000 people in the middle of a weekday afternoon to go to the central closet of the hotel for that perfume launch. And the only reason there was only 7,000 people at the fire marshal wouldn't let anybody else in the hotel. You won't even have to know what the ad said. If you have one drop of salesmanship blood in your veins, as soon as I tell you the headline, you will know that it couldn't fail. And it will underline for you the supreme importance of what we have already accomplished by showing this man and the rest of the outright headline. Here is the headline. Toberbord 9 squares under oath that the new perfume does not contain an illegal section of perfume. Then it said, wife of famous movie star agrees to give away 10,000 free samples of the new fragrance in order to prove that it's safe to wear in public. So we went with the rest of the copy there. I'm telling you, I got off an airplane in Los Angeles and got a cab driver talking. He said, why don't that stuff contain the legal section? You know, well, I was pretty sure of myself, but I wasn't completely sure. And the day of the perfume launch, I think it was Blake Thomas picking me up in the car. And I said, Blake, just go near the hotel and drive around it. There's a lot of people there taking me back home. We got there. They were just blind around the block. And when we had television coverage, radio coverage, we were written up in Time Magazine. We had unsolicited purchase orders from Federico, the biggest department store chain in the world, Burdak, Stiles, just on and on and on and on and on. Now, when I went in there, Tober still didn't know how this was made. And I had bought 13 staff liars, and I had them all in a nice little presentation case. And I had that presentation case handcuffed to some great big burly bodyguard type in Tephisto. There were two of them. And he went up and presented them to Tober, who then raffled them off to the audience. Because each of those staff liars commemorated one of the secret steps used in the making of Tober Cercurio. Let me tell you one of the reasons I always make people chant, all clients suck. And they do. I mean, if I were a client, I would suck. And if you were my client, you would suck. And there are more ways that it's going to go wrong. I guess it's like any other kind of relationship. But that one had a particular difficulty in it. And Tober made all of her decisions based on astrology. She had this astrologer named Signi Taff. And if Tober wanted to go to the bathroom, she'd call Signi and say, is it okay if I go to the bathroom now? Signi would say yes or no and charge her 100 bucks. Now, you probably think that's an exaggeration, but it's not much. We were hamstrung by the fact that Signi had to approve everything that we did. There was a point in time when we were not allowed to hire women or straight men. We could only hire gay men to work for the Tober Nye Corporation. That's what the astrological vibe said. When we began getting purchase orders and interest from federated, we didn't know anything about marketing to retail stores. All the expertise that my little crew was in direct response. So we found a woman who would be a perfect liaison for us, who was available. And Tova and Ernie, I would never forget, were staying at the Palace Hotel in Madison Avenue in Blade Thomas and I, with the little headquarters building back in Beverly Hills. We were so happy to gotten there early in the morning, got them on the phone, got them on the time chain, and said, have her charged. Have her charged. And so now, we had to call Signi Taff to see if it was okay for us to hire this person. And Signi said, didn't I tell you I don't want you hiring anymore? When I said, Signi, maybe this one's okay. Well, it wasn't, you know. So we didn't hire the women. How bad did this get to you, actually? I'm glad you asked that question. No, no, that's mild. Here's how bad it got. I'm sitting, I introduce her to the return. It's November, like the first week of November. I think her birthday was November 7th, but this is before the first day. The phone number, Tova writes down some numbers, hangs up the phone, looks up to the return of this charge. Do you have any idea of her rotten attitude, such as her latitude, such as her wrist? She's a rough-sickie, not strong, and that's where I'm supposed to be on my birthday. So she looks it up and it's in Belgium. Now she's got to go to Belgium. She's got to be there next week on her birthday to bathe in the right cognitive radiation. The problem was that Ernie was a very jealous guy and didn't like for her to travel and be away from him. Well, so she's got to figure out a way to get him to go. He doesn't like to travel. You know, he likes to golf and snatch and scratch his ass and sleep. So she does a little research and she finds out that right across the border from Belgium, in France, there's a guy named Duchodlai who specializes in taking garden varieties of sage, chopping them in half, putting television in bars, stuff like that, and stretching them out into a $40 million monstrosse. She calls Duchodlai and says, I want you to know something, that we can over there pay you cash for one of your products. You know, I'm Mrs. Ernie's foreman. When you go along with the little thing I want to pull off and tell him that you're giving us the car for free because you're such a thing and you want to be able to associate with the celebrity thing, I say, oh, we've got to go. Of course, you know, he's going to do that, right? For a quarter million dollar in cash, she was in. She calls Ernie, honey, you're not going to believe. I just got a call from this nice man, Duchodlai, and guess what he wants to do? And she caught Ernie into getting his fat ass on a plane, flying all the way to France, so that on her birthday, she could sneak across the border into Belgium and bathe in the right cosmic radiation. Now that's big time nuts, right? But I don't, I don't really care about that. If she wants to buy quarter million dollar cars, why should I care? But you know when I really started to care? Perfume launch, 7,000 people with more trying to get in the door. Publicity, time magazines, radio, TV, unsolicited things. I own a third of this project and it was stopped the next day because Zignitaft said it ain't the right time, cosmic place, to launch a new project. I swear to God, true story. That was a, it was a her. You know what I'm saying? But anyway, I believe the reason I told that story, at least in the beginning, was the idea of a headline. Can you imagine someone reading that headline and not wanting to know what the hell that's about? I mean everybody just did. So what I've just gotten you to do, a headline will come to you from this exact procedure. And we're going to take a break, but the one thing that I want to leave you with is the idea today, and I want you to really grasp this. We're not leaving any of this up to chance, are we? We are not waiting for inspiration. There is going to be no revelation from God. No one's going to appear from the heavens and do this for it. We are going to methodically, we are going to produce a-ha experiences, but we're, they're going to be intelligent a-ha experiences that have been based on good neurological imprinting of our brains from the good days. Okay. Now we're going to take a ten minute break and we'll come back and I'll tell you some more things.
